en.text: English spelling is a museum: silent letters preserve how words sounded centuries ago.

es.text: Spanish writes almost exactly as it sounds — but ñ, accents and the silent h keep their own rules.

fr.text: French keeps letters it no longer says: circumflexes mark an s that vanished long ago.

de.text: German builds long words by joining short ones — a whole idea in a single compound.

pt.text: Portuguese carries nasal vowels (ão, õe) that no other major European language spells this way.

it.text: Italian says every letter — and its doubled consonants are always heard, never silent.

nl.text: Dutch has a letter made of two: 'ij', written together and counted as one.

pl.text: Polish stacks consonants into clusters that look fierce but flow once you feel the rhythm.

sv.text: Swedish loves compounds and one famous sound — 'sj' — spelled a dozen different ways.

nb.text: Norwegian keeps three extra vowels — æ, ø, å — each its own clear sound.

tr.text: Turkish is almost perfectly phonetic, and its vowels harmonise so words sing in one key.

vi.text: Vietnamese quốc ngữ uses tone marks so one syllable can become six different words.

ko.text: Hangul was created in 1443 so everyone could learn to read — its letters mirror the mouth.

ja.text: Japanese kana were born from simplified kanji — a whole syllabary shaped from characters.

zh.text: Chinese characters are built from parts that picture meaning — a story inside each one.

fil.text: Filipino layers Spanish, Malay and English — its spelling tells the islands' history.
